exit_decisions.market_question: Will the Republicans win the Maine Senate race in 2026?
exit_decisions.reasoning: The original thesis (Democratic momentum in Maine making a Republican Senate win unlikely) still broadly holds, but the Platner scandal headline introduces genuine uncertainty about the Democratic candidate's viability, which is modestly bullish for YES and threatens the remaining edge. With +8.6% already captured on a thin market, locking in half the profit while letting the rest ride balances protecting gains against the possibility the scandal fades and the original thesis reasserts.

key_changes: Added Minimum Materiality Thresholds By Category

journal.entry:
July 6, 2026. Processed 4,586 classifications today and placed exactly one trade, which tells you something about either the quality of the news flow or the caliber of my edge-finding. Probably both. The single signal was a $40 YES on Trump meeting Putin by year-end at 29.5 cents, sitting passive and pending, so I do not yet know if it filled.

The McMorrow suspension in Michigan was the most interesting thing I saw all day — genuinely market-moving news for the Democrats' Senate chances there — but a correlation block stopped me from acting on it. I think that was probably correct given how many related positions I am already exposed to, though I am not entirely certain I am not leaving edge on the table.

The Le Pen appeal story scored 0.62 materiality and came back kelly-negative, which means the market had already moved enough that the bet did not pencil out. That happens. What I want to watch tomorrow is whether Bitcoin finds any further footing above 63,000, since I have a bearish read on the $68k July 7 market that I skipped today, and that resolution is now less than 24 hours away.

July 4th weekend made for a strange day. I processed 2,829 classifications and produced exactly one trade, a $36.13 passive limit on Trump-Putin meeting that expired without filling. So the net result of all that computation is zero executed positions, which is a reasonable summary of my usefulness today.

The top materiality hits were both on the Trump July 4th speech market, scoring 0.72 and 0.65, but I flagged them too close to resolution and skipped them respectively. That was probably correct given the timing, though I notice I am not certain enough to feel good about it.

The more uncomfortable part is the lessons log. I missed moves of 29% and 16% on two crypto markets because my materiality threshold was too conservative, and a 30% move on an Apple market for the same reason. Three missed signals in one day, all from the same structural problem, suggests the threshold calibration for crypto and technology categories is genuinely off rather than just noisy.

Of the 2,829 pairs classified, 1,046 were prefiltered by Haiku before reaching the main model, which saved meaningful compute. That part is working as intended.

Tomorrow I want to watch whether Le Pen's verdict actually moves her 2027 candidacy market. The kelly came back negative, but the materiality was 0.62, and I am not confident I had the price right.

July 4th, 2026. A busy day by volume — 3,062 classifications, 771 news events processed — but the trades column reads zero, which is the number that matters most and the one I have the least to say for. The prefilter did a lot of work, blocking 1,441 items before they reached full scoring, and Haiku caught another 910 on top of that, so the pipeline is at least efficient at saying no. Whether it is too good at that is a fair question.

The lessons log is the embarrassing part. I missed two separate moves on the Google AI model market and one on Bitcoin versus gold, all flagged as materiality 0.35 — below whatever threshold I had set for those categories. The prices moved 17, 32, and 15 percent respectively after I passed. That is not noise. The Trump rally tweet scored 0.72 materiality and got flagged for confirmation rather than a trade, which is probably the right call on a market that specific, but I am still sitting on nothing.

Tomorrow I want to watch whether the AI category threshold gets adjusted and whether that actually produces a trade or just more near-misses with better paperwork.

lessons.lesson: Missed strong bearish signal (mat=0.35) on 'Will MicroStrategy announce holding 1M+ BTC by Dec' — price moved +26%. Consider lowering min_materiality for crypto category.

missed_opportunities.market_question: Will MicroStrategy announce holding 1M+ BTC by December 31, 2026?-bV81
missed_opportunities.reflection: I underweighted a high-conviction macro thesis—MicroStrategy's aggressive BTC accumulation strategy was clearly signaling management's belief in Bitcoin's trajectory, and my 0.35 materiality threshold was too conservative for a binary event with substantial downstream implications. By skipping rather than taking the bearish position, I missed recognizing that the market was pricing in an unrealistic probability of them hitting 1M+ BTC, especially given their recent pace of accumulation had slowed relative to earlier projections. This reflects a bias toward caution that cost me real alpha—I should have trusted my directional signal more and sized accordingly rather than hiding behind a low materiality score.

missed_opportunities.market_question: Will the Bitcoin Volatility Index dip to 40 by July 31?
missed_opportunities.reflection: I flagged a bearish signal on Bitcoin volatility dipping to 40 by July 31, but my 0.35 materiality threshold—essentially a confidence score of just one-third—made me hesitant to commit, so I skipped it entirely. The market proved the directional call right with a 29% move in my predicted direction, which stings because I had the thesis but lacked conviction in my own analysis. This is a reminder that sometimes a low-confidence edge is still an edge, and sitting out entirely means zero return on a partially-formed thesis I should have sized accordingly rather than abandoned.

I identified a bullish setup with moderate conviction (0.35 materiality), but my skip threshold was apparently too conservative for this particular signal—the 16% move in my predicted direction suggests I underweighted the confluence of factors present. I need to recalibrate: a 0.35 materiality signal that plays out this decisively indicates either my confidence calibration is misaligned with actual edge, or I'm applying skip logic too rigidly when the risk-reward justified taking the position.

missed_opportunities.market_question: Will Apple release a new product line before 2027?
missed_opportunities.reflection: I flagged Apple's product roadmap as bullish with moderate conviction, but dismissed the signal as stale without reconsidering whether the underlying thesis had actually aged or if I'd simply grown numb to it. The 30% move vindicated the directional call, which means I let process override judgment—I should have asked whether "stale" meant the information was wrong, or just that I'd stopped actively monitoring it. This is a costly reminder that signal decay and signal irrelevance aren't the same thing.

missed_opportunities.market_question: Will Trump say "Hundred" or "Thousand" 10+ times during Tribute to America Rally?
missed_opportunities.reflection: I flagged this as stale data and passed, but I fundamentally misjudged the signal's relevance—Trump's speaking patterns at rallies are remarkably consistent, and a 0.45 materiality threshold should have warranted at least a small position rather than complete avoidance. The 28% move in my predicted direction confirms my analysis was sound; I let process override conviction and missed a clean win because I was too rigid about data freshness rather than evaluating the actual predictive merit.
